speaker: interviewer
text: --Israel, because it was just cut off.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't you say something?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now? Are you going to slate it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: November 8, '94, survivor Hart, Sigi. Interviewer, Deborah Kattler. Los Angeles, English. Fifth tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948, Israel was declared as Medinat Yisrael. And of course right away, the war started, all the Arabs country. And it was no doubt about that we are joining, and we became the Army. And I fought in the Galil, and was liberated from the Army in 1949-- '50, I was liberated from the Army, and went, of course, every-- every month we had to-- every year, we had to go one month. And then had a nice life. I worked in different jobs. I didn't have really a, a, a trade. Worked in different jobs. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Hanukkah of 1952, we made a bachelor Hanukkah party, and a friend of mine brought a girl, a French tourist to this party. And it-- I was the cook. I was cooking latkes. Everybody was dancing. And she peeked into kitchen, and I met her. I saw her. I fell in love right-- the first time I saw her. And she became my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Vera now. She was born Vera Vogel. But we got married in-- in August of '53. And '55 we had our son born. His name was Shlomi. And it was hard. It was hard work for me in Israel. I lost a few jobs because I went once a year to the Army, and there was no law that I am allowed to continue in my job. So whoever got my job, I had to look for something else. Until 1956, I worked on a taxicab. I was stopped in the street in 1956 to pick up soldiers from their house because they started in the Sinai War in 1956, in November. I think it was November or October. It was the Six Day War. This was my last fighting in Israel. And I said, I had already a little boy, and I didn't want to die in the desert. I want to continue living. I wrote to my sister, she sent me papers from America. And we left Israel in 1957. In February of '57, we left Israel, arrived in New York March 1. March 1, we arrived penniless to, to New York. I, I, I don't know why we just had 10-- $10 we could take out from Israel, but I had family-- the people who sent us papers in 1936 to Germany made a get-together and invited us, and they bought a ticket for us to continue to Los Angeles. We took a plane. My sister lives in Los Angeles, since 1944. The plane was taking us from New York to, to Los Angeles. It was a 12-hour flight then, by propeller. And I started to look around here for jobs. I didn't know how to get around. I didn't have a car. In Los Angeles, you have to drive. My wife had a, a lady, a lady, taking care of, her name de Rothschild, Baroness de Rothschild. My wife was in an orphanage in France, and this lady happened to be in and America at this time, and she knew she knew us. She invited us to her house. And she asked me if I have a job. I told her about the situation. She told me to come back a week later, and she had a car for me ready. She actually presented me with a car, so I can go to work and travel around. This was my first big break. And I worked around until I had a friend coming here from Israel, also. He was in the drapery business. He taught me a few tricks of the trade, how to install drapes. And I became very good at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I became a drapery installer, till someday I was hanging drapes. An acquaintance of mine asked me if I can get together $10,000. He is looking for a partner. At this time we were already living in a duplex, but we were paying slowly. And my wife said, yes, we will do it. I ask her, can we afford it, can we do it? She was always go, go for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We re-mortgaged our house. It was not enough. We borrowed from friends money. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and meantime, we had a second baby here, our daughter, Carmella. She was born in 1959, in February. And in 1967, I went into the manufacturing business as a partner. And we worked-- we did boys' and men's sport shirts, knit. And the company was called California Knitwear. And it was pretty successful for quite a while, until later on we had to switch over to, to, to contracting, and I got sick. So I had to retire from the-- fromt eh manufacturing. My partner, in the meantime, died. I am still partner with his wife. We have the property downtown, and this is from what we make our living, from the rental of this building.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is more or less my story. I have a wonderful family, and I am happy to be alive. Well, I know that we'll get a chance to meet some of your family. I have some questions. It sounds like there were many heroes that helped you and your family survive. And I was just wondering if you knew the names of the French family that helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The French people who kept us almost a year in their house, they are dead. The parents-- the children, who is now the man, Jano, and his sister, Jeanette. I went back in 1982 to make a visit. And this was after 40 years. They couldn't believe it. They thought we are dead. We met. It was a very, very emotional [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] reunion. And he called me 'my brother.' He-- and I visited with him three months ago, this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gazave. His name is Jano Gazave. His sister lives still in Luchon, and he lives near Toulouse, in a village called [? Gazare. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also mentioned the priest in Florence.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, my brother actually was taken care by a priest in a-- in a orphanage. He was with my friend, Louis Goldman, who wrote also a book about our-- our story-- was hidden in the Italian orphanage.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the name of that priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Giovanni. Giovanni, I remember the name Giovanni. He was very-- he came even once to Israel to visit with my brother, and we got-- this Giovanni passed away already. But it was a lot of the Italian people who helped mostly survive the Jews, and the French who were the Allied-- the Allied forces did everything possible to give out the Jews to the Germans. I mean, they did work, what they were not necessary to do, they did, to get rid of the Jews. That's all I can say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I know that your wife is here. I'd like you to introduce her.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to introduce my wife, Vera, for the last 41 years, my best friend and companion.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Vera, you are also a survivor?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I grew up in France. I was born and raised there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in an orphanage?

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war. [SOB]

speaker: interviewee
text: It's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sigi, did Vera have a large family? Vera--

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, we-- my parents came from Poland, and the entire family was in Poland. We were the only one in France. And there were six children. And they were deported. Nobody came back. From the three children-- there are three of us that are survivor, my brother in Paris, my sister in San Francisco, and I. And we never met anybody else, because everybody was living in Poland. However, after the war, we did-- some family looked for us. Some on my mother's side, like an aunt on my mother's side, they knew there were some children in France. And they looked for us, and they found us. And also from my father's side, only one sister. She lived in Paris, and actually, she's the one that helped us survive. And that's about it. We were small, and remember very little from before, just running, running, and then being caught by the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you were caught by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 11. I was 8 when the war started, and then after our parents were taken, there were just the three of us left. And my aunt, with the help of some Jewish organization in Paris, helped us. And we were hidden in Brittany by some gentile people who were not good to us. But my aunt helped us survive. She brought us back to Paris, and we stayed there during the entire war.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were hidden in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in-- just in her apartment. It's a coincidence. We were not living anywhere special. But the people just didn't denounce us. I mean, nobody knew us, but they knew my aunt. And she wore the yellow star for many years. They knew she was Jewish. But nobody bothered her. And one day she just took it off, because there were so few people in the street in Paris with the yellow stars. And we just stayed home. And there were maybe four, five, six children in a one-bedroom commercial unit, where she was working. And we slept on the table, underneath the table. We were all around, until the orphanage picked us up after the liberation of Paris. So then they gave us a good education. They helped us go back to school. That was the Rothschild family, which was very, very good to us. And we kept in contact for many years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we met [INAUDIBLE] in Israel. Her, her-- I think it's your aunt's cousin sent you from England to Israel to meet your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children are here now, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our children are here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I I would-- I would love for you to introduce them to us, as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. We shall do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you for sharing your stories with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: At this time, I want to introduce my family. You heard my wife just a few seconds ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Stephen. I'm the oldest son. I'm married to Tanya, and we have three kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm Carmella. And I'm married to Harry Birenbaum. And we have two kids, a boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, thank you for all being here at your father's testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. [INAUDIBLE] This is on the left-- on the left side, you have my grandfather. This picture was taken in Germany around 1934. And next to him--

speaker: interviewer
text: Name the names.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Meier Wulkan. And next to him is his wife, Simcha Wulkan. She had nine children. And you have the oldest son, Nathan, his younger brother, Jacob, and his little sister, Anna. On the left to is my mother, Adela. On the right this is my father, Herman. And this picture was taken about to 1922-ish. And they were just going to the theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sisters [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 1935. This was the wedding of my uncle, my mother's brother, Jacob, who got married to Freidel. And we were all at the wedding. This is me. This is my brother, Willy, and my, my mother, and my sister, and my father, and my two cousins on the bottom. They're in Israel. The rest, most of them, were killed by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken the end of April. I was sent by the Germans to [INAUDIBLE], where I gained 23 kilos, approximately 50 pounds. And this was the uniform we were wearing in the concentration camps. Of course, this is myself, Sigi Hart.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were sent by the Germans, or the French?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, by the French.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in May of 1945, shortly after I met my mother back in Toulouse. And this was taken for a souvenir. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1949, while I was in the Army. Also with a good friend of mine, who is now in New York, for a souvenir. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is me and Josef Bernstein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1946. Beginning on the left side, you can see this is me, Sigi. In the middle is our friend, Louis Goldman, and on the right is my brother, Wolf, Willy. [? Ludi ?] Goldman wrote a book about our escaping from France to Italy. This picture was taken as a souvenir. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1946 in Palestine, now Israel. On the left is Willy, my brother. In the middle is my mother, Adela, and I'm on the right-hand side. The picture was taken also as a souvenir. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is March 1 in New York, 1957. We arrived in New York, and my wife's sister, who is on the left, Michelle, and my wife, Vera, in the middle. And believe it or not, this is our Captain Hart, when he was a year old, when we came from Israel. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This picture was taken November 5, 1993, in Florence, Italy. It was a memorial on the day where the people were deported from Florence to Auschwitz. In the background, if you can see, there is a whole list of the people who went with me. And I was the only one back on this transport, taken in 1993 as a souvenir for me. My brother, my son. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is January 19, 1985, my daughter's wedding, Carmella. And from the left you have Stephen, then Vera, and then Harry, the khosn, and Carmella, the bride, and me on the side. And this was a wedding picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: We are together, to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in June of '94. On the left is Nicole Birenbaum, and on the right, her brother, Jeremy Birenbaum. These are the children from my daughter, Carmella, and Harry Birenbaum.

speaker: interviewer
text: These are your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] June 5, our son's wedding. On the left, it's me, Sigi. And then my wife, Vera. And then Tanya, his wife now, and Steve. This was their wedding, on [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] June 5, 1989. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] On this picture is-- it was taken and in August of 1994. And you have Brandon and the little baby, Jason, and Kevin. These are our son's children, and they're our grandchildren, of course. And this picture was taken for grandpa and grandma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Sigi, when you look at these pictures of your children and your grandchildren, and you think to their future, and your great-grandchildren, even, what kind of message would you want to tell them about your life and your experiences. If you could tell them something, what would it be?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would tell them to be strong, to be good Jews, never to deny that they're Jews. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and to be good people. Good people, strong people, not to let things push them down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you think back about the experiences of your life that you've shared with us, is there something about that that you would want to tell them, about the way that you've lived your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I hope that they never, ever will have anything near to go through what we went through during World War II, that they should never know about this kind of problems. To be good people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much for sharing your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
